questions.q: What is an n-gram language model and how does it estimate the probability of a word sequence?
options: It estimates probabilities by assuming the next word depends on the entire previous sequence
It uses a Markov assumption that the next word depends only on the previous n-1 words, enabling efficient probability estimation from training data
It requires neural networks to function
It assigns equal probability to all possible word sequences
questions.explanation: N-gram models use a Markov chain assumption: P(word_i | history) ≈ P(word_i | word_i-n+1, ..., word_i-1). This simplification makes them computationally tractable and allows probability estimation from relatively small datasets. Bigrams (n=2) and trigrams (n=3) are common.

questions.q: What problem do n-gram language models face with rare or unseen word sequences?
options: They assign infinite probability to all sequences
They assign zero probability to any sequence not seen in training data, which is problematic for generalization
N-gram models never encounter this problem
They automatically learn all possible sequences
questions.explanation: Zero probabilities for unseen sequences cause log-probability estimates to become undefined (log 0 = -∞). Smoothing techniques like Laplace smoothing or backoff methods address this by redistributing probability mass to unseen sequences.

questions.q: What is perplexity in language modeling, and what does a lower perplexity indicate?
options: Perplexity measures how confusing a model's predictions are; lower perplexity means higher entropy and more confusion
Perplexity measures the average negative log-probability per word on a test set; lower perplexity indicates better predictions and a better model
Perplexity is the number of parameters in a neural network
Perplexity has no relationship to model quality
questions.explanation: Perplexity = 2^(average cross-entropy). It represents the effective 'branching factor'—how many equally likely alternatives the model thinks exist at each step. Lower perplexity means the model assigns higher probability to the actual text, indicating better performance.

questions.q: How do neural language models differ from traditional n-gram models?
options: Neural models use word embeddings and compute probabilities through neural network layers, avoiding the need to explicitly store counts and handling longer dependencies
Neural models only work for short sequences while n-gram models scale to any length
N-gram models are always superior to neural models
Neural and n-gram models are functionally identical
questions.explanation: Neural language models use distributed representations (embeddings) and learned nonlinear functions to estimate probabilities. They can capture longer-range dependencies than fixed n-grams, though at higher computational cost. Both have practical applications depending on constraints.

questions.q: What is the next-token prediction objective, and why is it so effective for pretraining language models?
options: Predicting the next character in a sequence without using the previous context
Predicting the next word given the previous words; this creates a self-supervised learning objective that requires only raw text
Predicting which language a text is written in
Predicting whether text is grammatically correct
questions.explanation: Next-token prediction is self-supervised: the target label (next word) comes directly from text without manual annotation. This allows training on unlimited raw text. The model learns linguistic structure, semantics, and factual knowledge implicitly through this simple objective.

questions.q: What advantage do recurrent neural networks (RNNs) and Transformers offer over n-gram models for language modeling?
options: They use less memory than n-gram models
They can attend to or process variable-length context beyond fixed n-gram windows, capturing long-range dependencies
They cannot model sequences
They are slower than n-gram models on all tasks
questions.explanation: RNNs maintain a hidden state that can theoretically carry information from far back in the sequence. Transformers use attention to directly compare any positions. Both overcome the fixed context limitation of n-grams, though at higher computational cost.

questions.q: What is a masked language modeling objective, and how does it differ from next-token prediction?
options: Masked modeling randomly replaces words with a special token and predicts those replaced words, enabling bidirectional context unlike left-to-right next-token prediction
Masked modeling is identical to next-token prediction
Masked modeling only works for very short sequences
Masked modeling is outdated and not used in modern models
questions.explanation: Masked Language Modeling (used in BERT) randomly masks words and predicts them using both left and right context. Next-token prediction (used in GPT-style models) predicts left-to-right using only past tokens. MLM enables bidirectional understanding; next-token enables generation.

questions.q: Why can very large n-gram models (n=5 or n=6) become impractical despite using smoothing techniques?
options: Large n-grams are impossible to compute mathematically
The number of possible n-grams grows exponentially with n, requiring massive storage and data to estimate probabilities accurately
N-grams larger than 4 are forbidden by law
Large n-grams always produce perfect predictions
questions.explanation: The vocabulary size V raised to power n (V^n) grows exponentially. For n=6 and V=100,000, this becomes computationally infeasible. Most training data contains each specific 6-gram at most once, making reliable probability estimation difficult without astronomically large corpora.

questions.q: What role does the softmax function play in neural language models?
options: It removes noise from training data
It converts the model's output scores into a probability distribution over the vocabulary, enabling probability calculation for next-token prediction
It filters out common words from the training data
It compresses text into shorter sequences
questions.explanation: The softmax function transforms raw scores (logits) into a probability distribution where all values are positive, sum to 1, and represent relative likelihoods. This allows treating the language modeling objective as predicting a categorical distribution over vocabulary words.

questions.q: How does vocabulary size trade-off with language model performance?
options: Larger vocabulary always produces better models

Larger vocabularies increase the softmax layer's computational cost and the data required to learn good representations for rare words, while small vocabularies miss important words and harm expressiveness
Very small vocabularies (under 100 words) are always superior
Vocabulary size doesn't affect model performance
questions.explanation: Large vocabularies provide better coverage but increase softmax computation (O(V)) and require more training data for quality representations. Small vocabularies reduce computation and data requirements but lose expressiveness. Modern practice uses subword tokenization to balance these trade-offs.
